(Portuguese pronunciation: [[kaɾiˈɔkɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-Carioca.ogg "File:Br-Carioca.ogg") or [[kɐɾiˈɔkɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is a [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") used to refer to residents of the [city of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and their culture.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Demonym for anything related to the city of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil

For other uses, see [Carioca (disambiguation)](/wiki/Carioca_(disambiguation) "Carioca (disambiguation)").

Not to be confused with [Karaoke](/wiki/Karaoke "Karaoke").

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | **This article has multiple issues.** Please help **[improve it](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Carioca "Special:EditPage/Carioca")** or discuss these issues on the **[talk page](/wiki/Talk:Carioca "Talk:Carioca")**. *([Learn how and when to remove these messages](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | This article includes a list of [general references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#General_references "Wikipedia:Citing sources"), but **it lacks sufficient corresponding [inline citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources#Inline_citations "Wikipedia:Citing sources")**. Please help to [improve](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Reliability "Wikipedia:WikiProject Reliability") this article by [introducing](/wiki/Wikipedia:When_to_cite "Wikipedia:When to cite") more precise citations. *(June 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  |  | | --- | --- | |  | This article **may be [confusing or unclear](/wiki/Wikipedia:Vagueness "Wikipedia:Vagueness") to readers**. Please help [clarify the article](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify"). There might be a discussion about this on [the talk page](/wiki/Talk:Carioca "Talk:Carioca"). *(June 2017)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Carioca** (Portuguese pronunciation: [[kaɾiˈɔkɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-Carioca.ogg "File:Br-Carioca.ogg") or [[kɐɾiˈɔkɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is a [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") used to refer to residents of the [city of Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), in [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") and their culture.

Like other Brazilians, *Cariocas* speak [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"). The *carioca* accent and [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") (also simply called "*carioca*", see below) are one of the most widely recognized in Brazil, in part because [TV Globo](/wiki/TV_Globo "TV Globo"), the most popular TV network in Brazil, is headquartered in Rio de Janeiro. Thus, many Brazilian TV programs, from news and documentary to entertainment (such as the [telenovelas](/wiki/Telenovela "Telenovela")), feature *carioca*-acting and -speaking talent.

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carioca&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The original meaning of the term is controversial, maybe from [Tupi language](/wiki/Tupi_language "Tupi language") "*kari' oka*", meaning "white house" as the whitewashed stone houses of European settlers or even the colonists themselves, by merging "*kara'iwa*" (white man) and "*oka*" (house). Others propose the "beloved eyes" or "black eyes" meanings, derived from "kara" (beloved or black) and "oka" (eye). Currently, the more accepted origin in academia is the meaning derived from "*[kariîó oka](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Kari%C3%AEooka "wiktionary:Kariîooka")*", which comes from Tupi "house of [carijó](/wiki/Carij%C3%B3 "Carijó")", which was [Guaraní](/wiki/Guaran%C3%AD_people "Guaraní people"), a native [tribe](/wiki/Tribe "Tribe") of Rio de Janeiro who lived in the vicinity of the [Carioca River](/wiki/Carioca_River "Carioca River"), between the neighborhoods of *Glória* and *Flamengo*.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carioca&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Cariocas.jpg/250px-Cariocas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cariocas.jpg)

Cariocas

The archaic demonym for the state of Rio de Janeiro is *Fluminense*, taken from the Latin word *flūmen*, meaning "river". Despite the fact that *Carioca* is a more ancient demonym of Rio de Janeiro's inhabitants (known since 1502), it was replaced by *fluminense* in 1783, when the latter was sanctioned as the official demonym of the Royal Captainship of Rio de Janeiro (later the Province of Rio de Janeiro).

In 1763, the colonial capital of Brazil was transferred from [Salvador](/wiki/Salvador,_Bahia "Salvador, Bahia") to Rio de Janeiro. From 1783 and during the rest of the colonial period and then the [independent empire](/wiki/Empire_of_Brazil "Empire of Brazil"), *Carioca* remained only as a nickname by which other Brazilians called the inhabitants of Rio (city and province). During the first years of the Brazilian Republic, *Carioca* was the name given to those who lived in the slums or a pejorative way to refer to the bureaucratic elite of the [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_of_Brazil_(1891%E2%80%931960) "Federal District of Brazil (1891–1960)").

In 1960, when Brazil’s capital and the [Federal District](/wiki/Federal_District_(Brazil) "Federal District (Brazil)") were transferred from Rio de Janeiro to newly built [Brasília](/wiki/Bras%C3%ADlia "Brasília"), the city of Rio de Janeiro was reorganized as the state of [Guanabara](/wiki/Guanabara_(state) "Guanabara (state)"). During this transition, *Carioca* was recognized as a co-official demonym alongside *Guanabarino*, both referring to residents of the former capital.

In 1975, during the presidency of [Ernesto Geisel](/wiki/Ernesto_Geisel "Ernesto Geisel") under Brazil’s military dictatorship, the State of Guanabara was merged with the neighboring State of Rio de Janeiro. Following the merger, the city of Rio de Janeiro replaced [Niterói](/wiki/Niter%C3%B3i "Niterói") as the capital of the unified state, and *Carioca* became the official demonym for inhabitants of the city.

Nowadays, Carioca is used to exclusively refer to those born in the city of Rio de Janeiro, while everyone born in the state of Rio de Janeiro is referred to as a Fluminense.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Accomplishments and influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carioca&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Accomplishments and influence")]

See also: [List of people from Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/List_of_people_from_Rio_de_Janeiro "List of people from Rio de Janeiro")

*Carioca* people have invented a few sports; the most famous is [footvolley](/wiki/Footvolley "Footvolley").

*Cariocas* are credited with creating the [bossa nova](/wiki/Bossa_nova "Bossa nova") style of music.

Famous *Cariocas* in film include "Brazilian bombshell" [Carmen Miranda](/wiki/Carmen_Miranda "Carmen Miranda"), a Portuguese-born Brazilian woman who grew up in Rio de Janeiro. The eponymous song "[Carioca](/wiki/Carioca_(1933_song) "Carioca (1933 song)")", from the 1933 film *[Flying Down to Rio](/wiki/Flying_Down_to_Rio "Flying Down to Rio")*, has become a [jazz standard](/wiki/Jazz_standard "Jazz standard").

*Carnaval Carioca* is the Portuguese name for the largest [Brazilian Carnival](/wiki/Brazilian_Carnival "Brazilian Carnival"), the [Rio Carnival](/wiki/Rio_Carnival "Rio Carnival").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

*Samba Carioca* is a localized style of [Brazilian Samba](/wiki/Samba_(Brazilian_dance) "Samba (Brazilian dance)").

*How to be a Carioca* by [Priscilla Ann Goslin](/wiki/Priscilla_Ann_Goslin "Priscilla Ann Goslin") provides advice to visitors to the city on how to fit in with the local culture and lifestyle. It has sold over 350,000 copies since being first published in 1992 and provided the inspiration for a Portuguese television series of the same name that was released in 2023.[[3]](#cite_note-Graded-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Amazon-4)

There is an exercise drill used for dynamic stretching called *Carioca*. It consists of a repeating Samba dance step.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Sociolect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Carioca&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sociolect")]

| Carioca | |
| --- | --- |
| *Carioca*, *sotaque* | |
| Pronunciation | Portuguese pronunciation: [[kaɾiˈɔkɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Br-Carioca.ogg "File:Br-Carioca.ogg") or [[kɐɾiˈɔkɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese") |
| Region | [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro") |
| [Language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family") | [Indo-European](/wiki/Indo-European_languages "Indo-European languages")  * [Italic](/wiki/Italic_languages "Italic languages")   + [Romance](/wiki/Romance_languages "Romance languages")     - [Western Romance](/wiki/Western_Romance_languages "Western Romance languages")       * [Ibero-Romance](/wiki/Ibero-Romance_languages "Ibero-Romance languages")         + [West-Iberian](/wiki/West_Iberian_languages "West Iberian languages")           - [Galician-Portuguese](/wiki/Galician-Portuguese "Galician-Portuguese")             * [Portuguese](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language")               + [Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese_language "Brazilian Portuguese language")                 - Fluminense-Capixaba dialect                   * **Carioca** |
| [Writing system](/wiki/Writing_system "Writing system") | [Portuguese alphabet](/wiki/Portuguese_alphabet "Portuguese alphabet") |
| Language codes | |
| [ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3") | – |
| [IETF](/wiki/IETF_language_tag "IETF language tag") | `pt-u-sd-brrj` |

The Portuguese spoken across the states of Rio de Janeiro and [Espírito Santo](/wiki/Esp%C3%ADrito_Santo "Espírito Santo") and neighboring towns in [Minas Gerais](/wiki/Minas_Gerais "Minas Gerais") (and to a certain extent the city of [Florianópolis](/wiki/Florian%C3%B3polis "Florianópolis")), has similar features, hardly different from one another so cities such as [Paraty](/wiki/Paraty "Paraty"), [Resende](/wiki/Resende,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Resende, Rio de Janeiro"), [Campos dos Goytacazes](/wiki/Campos_dos_Goytacazes "Campos dos Goytacazes"), [Cachoeiro de Itapemirim](/wiki/Cachoeiro_de_Itapemirim "Cachoeiro de Itapemirim"), [Vila Velha](/wiki/Vila_Velha "Vila Velha") and [Linhares](/wiki/Linhares "Linhares") may be said to have the same dialect as Rio de Janeiro, as they are hardly perceived as strong regional variants by people from other parts of Brazil.

The [Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese "Brazilian Portuguese") variant spoken in the city of Rio de Janeiro (and metropolitan area) is called *Carioca*, and it is called *Sotaque* locally, literally translated as "accent". It can be said that Rio de Janeiro presents a [sociolect](/wiki/Sociolect "Sociolect") inside the major [Fluminense-Capixaba](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese#Deictics "Brazilian Portuguese") dialect, as speakers inside the city may be easily recognizable more by their slang than the way the phonology of their speech, which is closer to the standard Brazilian Portuguese in the media than other variants. It is known especially for several distinctive traits new to either variant (European or Brazilian) of the [Portuguese language](/wiki/Portuguese_language "Portuguese language"):

1. (for Brazilians) [Coda](/wiki/Syllable_coda "Syllable coda") /s/ and /z/ can be pronounced as [palato-alveolar](/wiki/Palato-alveolar_consonant "Palato-alveolar consonant") [[ʃ](/wiki/Voiceless_postalveolar_fricative "Voiceless postalveolar fricative")] and [[ʒ](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_fricative "Voiced postalveolar fricative")] of [English](/wiki/English_phonology "English phonology") or the [alveolo-palatal](/wiki/Alveolo-palatal_consonant "Alveolo-palatal consonant") [[ɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiceless alveolo-palatal fricative")] and [[ʑ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_fricative "Voiced alveolo-palatal fricative")] of [Catalan](/wiki/Catalan_phonology "Catalan phonology"). That is inherited from European Portuguese, and *Carioca* shares it only with [Florianopolitano](/wiki/Florianopolitan_dialect "Florianopolitan dialect") and some other *Fluminense* accents. In the northern tones of Brazilian Portuguese, not all coda /s/ and /z/ become postalveolar.
2. (for Europeans) /ʁ/, as well what would be coda /ɾ/ (when it is not pre-vocalic) in European Portuguese, may be realized as various voiceless and voiced [guttural-like sounds](/wiki/Guttural_R#Portuguese "Guttural R"), most often the latter (unlike in other parts of Brazil), and many or most of them can be part of the phonetic repertory of a single speaker. Among them the velar and uvular fricative pairs, as well both glottal transitions ([voiced](/wiki/Voiced_glottal_fricative "Voiced glottal fricative") & [unvoiced](/wiki/Unvoiced_glottal_fricative "Unvoiced glottal fricative")), the [voiceless pharyngeal fricative](/wiki/Voiceless_pharyngeal_fricative "Voiceless pharyngeal fricative") and the [uvular trill](/wiki/Uvular_trill "Uvular trill"): [[x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative")], [[ɣ](/wiki/Voiced_velar_fricative "Voiced velar fricative")] (between vowels), [[χ](/wiki/Voiceless_uvular_fricative "Voiceless uvular fricative")], [[ʁ](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative "Voiced uvular fricative")], [[h](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative")], [[ɦ](/wiki/Voiced_glottal_fricative "Voiced glottal fricative")], [[ħ](/wiki/Voiceless_pharyngeal_fricative "Voiceless pharyngeal fricative")] and [[ʀ](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_trill "Voiced uvular trill")].[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) That diversity of [allophones](/wiki/Allophones "Allophones") of a single [rhotic](/wiki/Rhotic_consonant "Rhotic consonant") phoneme is rare not just in Brazilian Portuguese but among world languages.
3. (for both) The consonants /t/ and /d/ before /i/ or final unstressed /ɛ ~ e/ ([[e̞](/wiki/Mid_front_unrounded_vowel "Mid front unrounded vowel")], that in this position may be raised to [i] or deleted) become affricates [[tʃ](/wiki/Voiceless_postalveolar_affricate "Voiceless postalveolar affricate") ~ [tɕ](/wiki/Voiceless_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiceless alveolo-palatal affricate")] and [[dʒ](/wiki/Voiced_postalveolar_affricate "Voiced postalveolar affricate") ~ [dʑ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolo-palatal_affricate "Voiced alveolo-palatal affricate")] (again, as those of English or Catalan, depending on the speaker), respectively. Originally probably from Tupi influence,[[9]](#cite_note-9) through the Portuguese post-creole that appeared in southeastern Brazil after the ban of [Língua Geral Paulista](/wiki/L%C3%ADngua_Geral_of_S%C3%A3o_Paulo "Língua Geral of São Paulo") as a marker of Jesuit activity by the Marquis of Pombal, this is now common place in Brazilian Portuguese, as it spread with the *[Bandeiras Paulistas](/wiki/Bandeirantes "Bandeirantes")*, expansion of *Mineiros* to the Center-West and mass media. It is not as universal in São Paulo, Espírito Santo and southern Brazil even though they were populated mostly by the original *bandeirantes* ([caboclos](/wiki/Caboclo "Caboclo"), formerly [Língua Geral](/wiki/L%C3%ADngua_Geral "Língua Geral") speakers) because the European immigrants learning Portuguese and their descendants preferred more conservative registers of the language, perhaps as a mark of a separate social identity. The Northeast had Nheengatu, another Língua Geral, too, but it had a greater native Portuguese-speaker presence, had a greater contact with the colonial metropolis and was more densely populated.
4. (for both) Historical [[ɫ](/wiki/Velarized_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Velarized alveolar lateral approximant")] (/[l](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_lateral_approximant "Voiced alveolar lateral approximant")/ in syllable coda), which merged with coda /[ɾ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_tap_or_flap "Voiced alveolar tap or flap")/ ([[ɻ](/wiki/Voiced_retroflex_approximant "Voiced retroflex approximant")]) in *[Caipira](/wiki/Caipira_dialect "Caipira dialect")*, has undergone labialization to [lʷ], and then [vocalized](/wiki/L-vocalization "L-vocalization") to [[u̯](/wiki/Close_back_rounded_vowel "Close back rounded vowel")];[[10]](#cite_note-bisol(2005)p211-10) Nevertheless, with the exception of [[ʊ̯](/wiki/Near-close_near-back_rounded_vowel "Near-close near-back rounded vowel")] being used in [Southern Brazil](/wiki/South_Region,_Brazil "South Region, Brazil") and [São Paulo](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Paulo_(state) "São Paulo (state)") instead of [u̯], both commonly transcribed as [[w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")], the process is now nearly ubiquitous in Brazilian Portuguese so only some areas retain velarized lateral alveolar approximant (rural areas close to the frontier with Uruguay) or the [retroflex approximant](/wiki/Retroflex_approximant "Retroflex approximant") (a very few *caipira* areas) as coda /l/.

The traits (particularly the *chiado*, a [palatalization](/wiki/Palatalization_(sound_change) "Palatalization (sound change)") process that creates a postalveolar pronunciation of coda *s* and *z* and affricate pronunciation of [ti] and [di] and *te* and *de* [rhymes](/wiki/Syllable#Rime "Syllable")), as a whole and consistent among the vast majority of speakers, were once specifically characteristic of Rio de Janeiro speech and distinguished particularly from the pronunciation of São Paulo and areas further south, which formerly had adapted none of the characteristics. The *chiado* of the coda sibilant is thought to date from the early 1800s occupation of the city by the Portuguese royal family, as European Portuguese had a similar characteristic for the postalveolar codas.

More recently, however, all of the traits have spread throughout much of the country by the cultural influence of the city that diminished the [social marker](/wiki/Social_marker "Social marker") character the lack of palatalization once had (a part of assimilation of the caboclo minorities in most of South and Southeast Brazil). Affrication is today widespread, if not nearly omnipresent among young Brazilians, and coda guttural r is also found nationwide (their presence in Brazil is a general heritage of Tupi speech too) but less among speakers in the 5 southernmost states other than Rio de Janeiro, and if accent is a good social indicator, 95-105 million Brazilians consistently palatalize coda sibilant in some instances (but as in Rio de Janeiro, it is only a marker of adoption of foreign phonology at large in Florianópolis and Belém: palatalization, as in any other Romance language, is a very old process in Portuguese and its lacking in some dialect rather than reflecting a specific set of Galician, Spanish and indigenous influences on their formation).

Another common characteristic of *Carioca* speech is, in a stressed final syllable, the addition of /j/ before coda /s/ (*mas*, *dez* may become [majʃ], [dɛjʃ], which can also be noted ambiguously as [mɐ̞ⁱʃ], [dɛⁱʃ]). The change may have originated in the Northeast, where pronunciations such as *Jesus* [ʒeˈzujs] have long been heard. Also immigration from Northeastern Brazil and Spanish immigration causes debuccalization of the coda sibilant: *mesmo* [meɦmu]. Many Brazilians assume that is specific to Rio, but in the Northeast, debuccalization has long been a strong and advanced phonological process that may also affect onset sibilants /s/ and /z/ as well as other consonants, primarily [v].

There are some grammatical characteristics of this sociolect as well, an important one is the mixing of second person pronouns *você* and *tu*, even in the same speech. For instance, while [normative](/wiki/Normative "Normative") Portuguese requires *lhe* as the oblique for *você* and *te* as oblique for *tu*, in *Carioca* slang, the once formal *você* (now widespread as an informal pronoun in many Brazilian Portuguese varieties) is used for all cases. In informal speech, the pronoun *tu* is retained, but with the verb forms belonging to the form você: *Tu foi na festa?* ("Did you go to the party?"). So the verbal forms are the same for both *você* and *tu*.

Many *Cariocas* and many *Paulistas* (from the coast, capital city or hinterland) shorten *você* and use *cê* instead: *Cê vai pra casa agora?* ("Are you going home now?"). That, however, is common only on the spoken language and is rarely written.

Slang words among youngsters from Rio de Janeiro include *caraca!* (gosh!) [now spread throughout Brazil], *e aê?* and *qualé/quaé/coé?* (literally "which is [it]", carrying a meaning similar to "What's up?"), *maneiro* ("cool", "fine", "interesting", "amusing"), *mermão* ("bro", contraction of *meu irmão*), *caô* (a lie), and *sinistro* (in standard Portuguese, "sinister"; in slang, "awesome," "terrific," but also "terrible," "troublesome," "frightening," "weird"). Many of these slang words can be found in practically all of Brazil by to cultural influence from the city. Much slang from Rio de Janeiro spreads across Brazil and may be not known as originally from there, and those less culturally accepted elsewhere are sometimes used to shun not only the speech of a certain subculture, age group or social class but also the whole accent.
